1: Isaiah 28:1 begins the third major woe-section (Isaiah 28-33) with the woe against Ephraim. 'Hoi ateret ga'ut shikkurei Efraim, woe to the proud crown of Ephraim's drunkards.' The 'proud crown' (ateret ga'ut, crown of pride) = Samaria, built on a hill in the fertile valley of the northern Kingdom — visible from a great distance, a commanding position. 'Whose glorious beauty is a fading flower' (tsits nevel tiferet tsiveo, a fading blossom, his glorious beauty) — the beautiful blossom that fades quickly. 'Al-rosh gei-shemanim, on the head of the valley of fat things' (gei-shemanim = the fertile valley, perhaps the Jezreel valley or the valley around Samaria). 'Haloumim yayin, struck down by wine' = the political/military elite overcome by their own pleasure. 722 BCE: the Assyrian siege and fall of Samaria is the fulfillment.
2: 'See, the Lord has a powerful agent — like a devastating hailstorm, like a destructive tempest, like a flood of overwhelming waters — who will smash it to the ground' — Assyria as YHWH's instrument: 'hineh chazaq ve'ammitz laAdonay, behold one who is mighty and strong for the Lord.' The three weather images for Assyrian military power: (1) ze'rem barad, a devastating hailstorm — hail that destroys crops; (2) sa'ar qataev, a destructive storm; (3) ze'rem mayim kabirim shotfim, a flood of overwhelming rushing waters — the same 'overwhelming flood' imagery as Isaiah 8:7-8 (the Assyrian river overflowing). 'Hieniach la'aretz beyad, he will put it down to the earth by force' — Samaria smashed to the ground by the overwhelming agent.
3: 'The proud crown of Ephraim's drunkards will be trampled underfoot' — the trampling of the proud crown: 'beraglaim titbosas ateret ga'ut shikkurei Efraim, by feet it will be trampled — the proud crown of Ephraim's drunkards.' The 'pride' crown literally trampled underfoot by the Assyrian military (who marched through Israel). The reversal of the crown-as-symbol: what is worn on the head (the highest point) is thrown to the ground and trampled (the lowest). The same reversal as Isaiah 25:12 (Moab's high walls brought to the dust) and Isaiah 26:5-6 (the lofty city trampled by the poor).
4: 'The beautiful crown on the head of the fertile valley will be a fading flower, like a first-ripe fig before summer: whoever spots it snatches and swallows it on the spot' — the eagerness of the conqueror: 'vehayeta tiferet navlato asher al-rosh gei-shmanim kebikkurah bilah, the fading flower of his beauty that is on the head of the fat valley will be like the early fig before summer.' The bikkurah (early fig) ripens before the main crop and is immediately eaten by whoever spots it. 'Asher yireh haro'eh othah beoddah vekappo yivalteenah, which whoever sees picks it while it is still in his hand and swallows it' — immediate consumption. Samaria's wealth attracted the Assyrian conqueror who 'swallowed' it immediately. The early-fig imagery = premature destruction before the full harvest of the kingdom's potential.
5: 'In that day the LORD of hosts will be a glorious crown and a beautiful diadem for the remnant of his people' — the positive vision for the remnant: 'bayom hahu yihyeh YHWH tseva'ot la'ateret tiferet ulitstil tiferet, in that day YHWH of hosts will be a crown of glory and a diadem of beauty.' YHWH himself replaces the proud crown of Ephraim (Isaiah 28:1) — not a crown worn by humans but YHWH as the crown worn by his people. 'For the remnant of his people' (lishe'ar ammo) — the remnant theology: those who survive judgment receive YHWH himself as their glory-crown. The contrast: Ephraim's crown was fading (Isaiah 28:1, 4); YHWH as crown is eternal glory.
6: 'And he will be a spirit of justice for those who sit in judgment, and strength for those who repel the battle at the gate' — the practical governance of the messianic era: 'ulruach mishpat layoshev al-hammishpat, and a spirit of justice to those who sit in judgment.' The spirit of justice (ruach mishpat = the judicial Spirit of God empowering the judges — the same sevenfold Spirit of Isaiah 11:2: 'Spirit of counsel and strength, Spirit of knowledge and fear of YHWH') enabling right judgment. 'Uligevurah mshivei milchamah sha'arah, and strength to those who push back the battle at the gate.' The gate-defenders (those who repel the military threat at the city gate) empowered by divine strength. The two functions: just governance and effective defense.
7: 'But these too stumble through wine and stagger from strong drink: priest and prophet are overcome by strong drink, confused by wine, reeling from intoxication — they err in their visions and stumble in their judgments' — the indictment of Jerusalem's religious leadership. 'Vegam-eleh bayayin shagu, but also these err because of wine' — the 'also these' (gam-eleh) connects the Jerusalem leadership to the drunken Ephraimites of Isaiah 28:1-4: the same problem south as north. Priests (kohen) and prophets (navi) both overcome (balu, swallowed/overwhelmed) by wine and strong drink. 'Tagu baro'eh paqeku mispat, they err in the vision/seeing and stumble in judgment' — the prophets' visions (ro'eh = seer) and the priests' legal judgments (mishpat) both compromised by drunkenness. The religious establishment that should provide the correction for Ephraim's pride is equally corrupt.
8: 'All the tables are covered with vomit and filth; there is no clean space anywhere' — the physical evidence of the drunken leadership: 'ki kol-shulchanot male'u qi tso'ah, for all the tables are full of vomit/vomited material and filth, bli-maqom, no place (is clean).' The sacred table of the covenant meal (Exodus 24:11: Moses and the elders ate before YHWH; the Bread of the Presence on the table in the tabernacle) is now covered in vomit. The sacred/profane reversal is complete: what should be the place of divine communion is the site of defilement. The physical description is viscerally disgusting by design — the shock value conveys the moral seriousness of the leadership's failure.
9: 'Who is he trying to teach? Who is he trying to make understand his message? Babies just weaned from milk, infants just taken from the breast?' — the mocking dismissal of the prophet by the corrupt leadership: 'et-mi yore dea, whom is he (the prophet/YHWH) teaching knowledge?' The 'who' is dismissive — the drunk priests and prophets mock the prophetic instruction as baby-talk. 'Ve'et-mi yavin shemu'ah, and who is he making to understand what he hears?' 'Gamelei mechalav atiqei mishadayim, those just weaned from milk, those just taken from breasts?' — the mockers compare the prophet's listeners to weaned infants, implying the prophetic message is too elementary for sophisticated adults. But the mockery backfires (Isaiah 28:13): the 'baby-talk' they dismiss will become the incomprehensible Assyrian speech that judges them.
10: 'For it is rule on rule, rule on rule; line on line, line on line; a little here, a little there' — the 'tsav latsav' verse: 'ki tsav latsav tsav latsav, qav laqav qav laqav, ze'er sham ze'er sham.' This is either (1) a mockery of Isaiah's teaching as repetitive babble (the leaders imitating what they perceive as Isaiah's boring elementary instruction), or (2) Isaiah quoting the drunken prophets' own slurred speech, or (3) Hebrew transliteration of what Assyrian speech sounds like to Israelite ears. All three interpretations converge: the mocking of prophetic instruction will be answered by foreign speech (Isaiah 28:11). The onomatopoetic quality — tsav tsav, qav qav — mimics the sound of incomprehensible or mocking speech.
11: 'For by people of strange lips and a foreign tongue the LORD will speak to this people' — the judgment-through-foreign-speech oracle: 'ki bela'aze safah uvelashon acheretm yedabber el-ha'am hazeh, for through stammerers of lip and through a different tongue he will speak to this people.' The people who mocked prophetic instruction as baby-talk will be spoken to in an actual foreign language (Assyrian) they cannot understand. Paul quotes Isaiah 28:11-12 in 1 Corinthians 14:21-22 to discuss speaking in tongues: 'Through people of strange tongues and through the lips of foreigners I will speak to this people, but even then they will not listen to me, says the Lord. Tongues, then, are a sign, not for believers but for unbelievers.' The tongue-as-judgment for unbelief becomes the tongue-as-sign in the NT context.
12: 'He said to them: This is the place of rest; give rest to the weary; this is the refreshing. But they refused to listen' — the rejected invitation: 'asher amar alehem, who said to them: zot hamenuchah, this is the rest.' YHWH had offered rest (menuchah = the deep Sabbath-rest of covenant security) to the 'weary' (yeaef, the exhausted/wearied = the covenant community burdened by Assyrian pressure). 'Vezot hamargea'ah, and this is the refreshment' (margeah = rest, relief). 'Velo avu shimoa, but they refused to hear.' The refusal of divine rest echoes the Wilderness generation's failure to enter the rest (Psalms 95:11: 'they shall never enter my rest'; Hebrews 3:11; Isaiah 4:1-10: the Sabbath-rest that remains for the people of God).
13: 'So the word of the LORD will become to them: rule on rule, rule on rule; line on line, line on line; a little here, a little there — so that they stumble backward, are broken, snared, and captured' — the word becomes judgment: 'vehayah lahem davar YHWH, and the word of YHWH will become to them: tsav latsav...' The same 'tsav latsav' mocking formula of Isaiah 28:10 returns — but now it is YHWH's word becoming to them the very incomprehensibility they mocked. The outcome: 'lema'an yelechu venichshelu achor venishberu venoqushu venilkadu, so that they stumble backward, are broken, snared, and captured.' The four verbs of progressive disaster: stumble → break → snare → capture = total judgment. The word they mocked becomes the instrument of their destruction.
14: 'So hear the word of the LORD, you scoffers who rule this people in Jerusalem' — the direct address to the Jerusalem ruling class: 'lakhen shime'u debar YHWH anshei latson, therefore hear the word of YHWH, O men of scoffing.' The latson (scoffing/mocking) is the specific sin of Isaiah 28:9-10 — those who mockingly dismissed prophetic instruction as baby-talk. 'Moshle ha'am hazeh asher biYerushalayim, those who rule this people in Jerusalem.' The ruling class (judges, counselors, senior officials) who have the responsibility of governing Jerusalem are specifically addressed. Their scoffing is not merely impolite — it is the political decision-maker's rejection of prophetic counsel, which will prove catastrophic.
15: 'You have said: We have made a covenant with death; with Sheol we have an agreement. When the overwhelming flood sweeps through, it will not touch us — for we have made lies our shelter and hidden ourselves under falsehood' — the 'covenant with death': 'karam berit et-mavet, for you have said: we have cut a covenant with death.' The diplomatic language (karat berit = to cut a covenant, the standard treaty formula) applied to a supposed alliance with death itself. The 'agreement with Sheol' (chozeh im-she'ol, a vision/pact with Sheol) = the belief that they have arranged exemption from judgment. The 'overwhelming flood' (shetef, the sweeping/overflowing flood = Assyrian conquest) 'will not reach us' (ki im-yaavor lo yevoinenu, for when it passes it will not come to us). Their protection: 'samnu chazav machsenu, we have made a lie our refuge; uvasheker nistarenu, and in falsehood we have hidden.' Their diplomatic arrangements (Egyptian alliance? political maneuvering?) built on falsehood.
16: 'So this is what the Lord GOD says: See, I am laying a stone in Zion — a tested stone, a precious cornerstone, a sure foundation. Whoever believes will not make haste' — the cornerstone oracle, the most NT-quoted verse in Isaiah's foundation section. 'Hineni yissad beTziyon even even bochan pinnat yiqrat muyyasar, behold I lay in Zion a stone, a stone of testing, a precious cornerstone, a sure foundation.' The four attributes: bochan (tested), yiqrat (precious/valued), pinnat (corner), muyyasar (sure/established foundation). 'Hama'amin lo yachish, he who believes/trusts will not make haste/panic' (lo yachish = will not flee in panic, will not be in anxious haste). Quoted in Romans 9:33 and Isaiah 10:11 (Paul adds 'will not be put to shame' from Psalms 118:22); 1 Peter 2:6-8 (combined with Psalms 118:22 and Isaiah 8:14). The Qumran community (1QS 8.7-8) applied this to the community itself as the living cornerstone.
17: 'I will measure with justice as my plumb line and righteousness as my level. The hail will sweep away your refuge of lies, and the flood will overwhelm your hiding place' — the divine building-inspection using justice and righteousness: 'vesamti mishpat leqav utsedaqah lemishqelet, and I will make justice the measuring line and righteousness the plumb line.' The measuring line (qav) and plumb line (mishqelet) are the builder's tools for checking whether a structure is true and straight — if justice and righteousness are the standard, the 'covenant with death' fails the inspection. 'Ubarad yikchah machaseh-chazav, and hail will sweep away the refuge of lies.' The hail (barad, the hail that destroys crops = divine judgment) 'sweeps away' the lie-refuge. 'Vemayim yishtofu miseter, and water will overwhelm the hiding place' — the flood they claimed would not touch them does.
18: 'Your covenant with death will be cancelled, and your agreement with Sheol will not hold. When the overwhelming flood sweeps through, you will be crushed under it' — the nullification of the death-covenant: 'vekuppar britekhemm et-mavet, your covenant with death will be annulled/wiped out' (kappar = to wipe out/atone = the atonement-removal vocabulary applied to the annulment of their illusory covenant). 'Vehazutekhemm et-she'ol lo taquom, and your vision/pact with Sheol will not stand.' The Assyrian 'overwhelming flood' they thought would bypass them: 'veam-yaavor shetef vehiyitem lo lemirmas, and when the sweeping flood passes, you will become trampled by it.' The very flood they made a covenant to avoid becomes what tramples them.
19: 'Every time it sweeps through, it will seize you. Morning after morning it will come — by day and by night. The very understanding of what it means will be terror' — the relentless repetition of judgment: 'mideiy avoro yiqqach etkhem, as often as it passes it will take/seize you.' The frequency: 'boqer boqer ya'abor bayom uvalayelah, morning by morning it will pass, by day and by night.' The unceasing morning-by-morning quality of the judgment — no rest between waves. 'Vehayah raq ziggavon havin shemu'ah, and it will be nothing but sheer terror to understand the report/message.' The 'understanding' (havin, discerning) of what is happening produces only terror (ziggavon = trembling). The moment of comprehension is the moment of terror — they finally understand what was prophesied but too late to respond.
20: 'The bed will be too short to stretch out on, and the covering too narrow to wrap yourself in' — the proverb of inadequate comfort: 'ki-qatsar hammatsea mehishtaream, for the bed is too short to stretch out on' (qatsar = too short). 'Vehammasekha tsarah lehitkannef, and the covering is too narrow to wrap yourself in.' The inadequate bed and narrow blanket are a vivid image of the futility of the 'covenant with death' — the arrangements they made to protect themselves are like trying to sleep on a too-short bed with a too-narrow blanket. There is no comfortable security in the false covenant. The proverb-like quality suggests an existing saying that Isaiah applies to the political situation.
21: 'For the LORD will rise up as he did at Mount Perazim; he will be roused as in the valley of Gibeon, to carry out his task — his strange task — his work — his alien work' — the two historical YHWH-warrior victories invoked: 'ki keBEhar Peratsim yaqum YHWH, for as on Mount Perazim YHWH will rise up.' Mount Perazim = Baal-Perazim (2 Samuel 5:20: 'YHWH broke through my enemies before me like a bursting flood' — David's victory over the Philistines). 'And he will rage as in the valley of Gibeon' (kaemeq begi'on yirgaz, he will be roused as in the valley of Gibeon) = Joshua 10:10-14 (the sun stood still, YHWH fought for Israel against the Amorites). But now YHWH fights against his own people: 'laashot ma'asehv zarr ma'asehv, to do his work, his alien work.' His 'strange/alien work' (zarr = alien/strange) = fighting against Israel is alien to his nature as Israel's champion. Lamentations 2:4-5 describes this: 'he has strung his bow... he has slain all who were pleasing to the eye; he has poured out his wrath like fire.'
22: 'Now stop your mocking, or your bonds will grow tighter. I have heard from the Lord GOD of hosts a decree of complete destruction against the whole earth' — the urgent warning: 've'attah al-titshotzatsu pen-yechezequ moseireikhem, so now do not mock or your bonds/chains will grow tighter' (chazaq, become strong/tighten). The mocking of Isaiah 28:9-10 and Isaiah 28:14 is what is addressed: the scoffers are warned that continued mocking will tighten the bonds of their judgment. 'Ki khalah venecharatset shamati me'et adonay YHWH tseva'ot, for a decree of complete destruction I have heard from the Lord YHWH of hosts' (khalah venechratseth = complete destruction and determined judgment, the same vocabulary as Isaiah 10:23: 'he will carry out his decreed destruction'). The universal scope: 'al-kol-ha'arets, against the whole land.'
23: 'Listen and hear my voice; pay attention and hear what I say' — the introduction to the parable: 'haazinu vishme'u qoli, listen and hear my voice.' The double call for attention (haazin = lend your ear; shema = hear) before the agricultural parable that follows. The parable (Isaiah 28:23-29) uses the farmer's wisdom to illustrate YHWH's differentiated, purposeful use of judgment. The structural function: after the woe-oracles against Ephraim and Jerusalem (Isaiah 28:1-22), the parable explains that divine judgment operates like expert farming — with different treatments for different conditions, none of them random or excessive.
24: 'Does a farmer spend the whole day plowing in order to plant? Does he keep breaking up the clods of his field forever?' — the first question of the parable: 'hayom kol-hayom yachresh hacharesh, does the farmer plow the whole day?' The rhetorical question expects 'no': plowing is a preparatory step, not an end in itself. 'Yiftach viyesaded admato, does he keep opening and harrowing his land?' (yesaded = break clods/harrow). The farmer does not plow endlessly — he plows for a purpose (planting) and then stops. The theological application: YHWH's judgment (plowing = breaking up) has a purpose and a limit. He does not judge forever — judgment prepares the soil for planting.
25: 'When he has leveled the surface, he broadcasts caraway, scatters cumin, plants wheat in rows, barley in its proper place, and spelt along its edge' — the different planting methods for different seeds: 'halo im-shivah panav, does he not, when he has leveled its surface' (shivah = made smooth/level). 'Vehefitz qetsach, broadcast caraway (black cummin/nigella sativa)' — scattered broadcast. 'Vekhamon yizkeh, and scatter caraway' — in rows. 'Vesam chittah, and plant wheat' in rows (sorek = in a row). 'Use'orah nismam, and barley in its proper place' (nismam = in its designated area). 'Vekussemeth gevulato, and spelt along its border' (gevulato = its boundary/edge). Each grain has its optimal planting method — broadcast for small aromatic seeds, row-planting for large grains. The diversity of method reflects expertise.
26: 'His God instructs him in the right way and teaches him' — the divine source of agricultural wisdom: 'veyissaro lemishpat Elohav yorennu, and his God instructs him in the right/justice of it; his God teaches him.' The farmer's expertise is YHWH's teaching (yorennu from yarah = to teach, the same root as Torah). The agricultural wisdom embedded in creation (the soil's response to different treatments, the optimal conditions for different crops) is YHWH's teaching built into the creation order. The farmer 'listens to' creation's instruction = listens to YHWH's instruction through nature. James 1:5 ('If any of you lacks wisdom, ask God, who gives generously to all') and Prov 8 (Lady Wisdom present at creation) are the same theology.
27: 'Caraway is not threshed with a sledge, nor does a cartwheel roll over cumin. Caraway is beaten with a stick, and cumin with a rod' — the differentiated threshing methods: 'ki lo becharuts yudash qetsach, for with a threshing sledge caraway is not threshed' (charuts = a heavy threshing sledge with iron teeth that would destroy delicate seeds). 'Veofan agalah al-kamon, and a cartwheel does not roll over cumin.' Both aromatic seeds (qetsach = nigella sativa; kamon = cumin) are too delicate for heavy processing. 'Ki vechamel yuchat qetsach vehakamon beshevet, rather caraway is beaten with a stick and cumin with a rod' — gentle, precise processing appropriate to the seed. The theological application: YHWH uses the right instrument with the right force for the right crop — Israel's judgment is proportionate discipline, not the crushing judgment of the nations.
28: 'Grain for bread must be crushed, but a farmer does not go on threshing it forever. He drives his cartwheel over it and lets horses trample it, but he does not grind it to powder' — the limit of threshing: 'lechem yudak, bread grain must be crushed' (yudak = must be beaten/crushed — the process is necessary). 'Ki lo lanetsach yidush lo, for he does not thresh it forever.' The farmer stops threshing at the right point — enough to separate grain from chaff, not so much as to grind the grain to powder. 'And he drives his horse-cart wheel over it but his horses do not grind it' (lo yedaqqenu, he does not crush it fine). The boundary of judgment: sufficient to accomplish the purpose (separate grain from chaff = separate the righteous remnant from the chaff of apostasy) but not excessive (not grinding to powder = not annihilation).
29: 'All of this too comes from the LORD of hosts, whose counsel is wonderful and whose skill is great' — the closing attribution of agricultural wisdom to divine wisdom: 'gam-zot me'im YHWH tseva'ot yatsa, this too comes from YHWH of hosts.' The farmer's differentiated wisdom mirrors YHWH's differentiated governance of history. 'Hipli etsah, he has made wonderful counsel' (pele' etsah = wonderful plan/counsel — pele' is the same root as the Wonderful Counselor of Isaiah 9:6). 'Higdil toushiyah, he has made great wisdom/skill' (toushiyah = productive wisdom, practical expertise that achieves its purpose). The parable's conclusion: YHWH's apparently harsh judgments are the work of the master-farmer who knows exactly how much threshing each crop needs and never applies more force than the purpose requires.
